topic.title: Uber || SDE || OT August 2022 || On Campus (with photos)

post.content:
Couldn't came up with an approach for Q3. Can anyone help? And also if someone knows the efficient version of Q1 and Q2, please share. 
# **q1. variant of (312. Burst Balloons)**
![image](https://assets.leetcode.com/users/images/17ae9680-821d-44c1-97ee-05948d14c8b0_1659789737.4691377.jpeg)
# **q2. variant of (63. Unique Paths II) with an edge case (see last part of the description)**
![image](https://assets.leetcode.com/users/images/ded856be-351f-4875-af1c-82ae574c4304_1659789815.1716044.jpeg)
# **q3. **
![image](https://assets.leetcode.com/users/images/d1270638-74f0-4a9b-a672-045249e4ffae_1659789873.2675302.jpeg)

Q3: combinations:

for a num x in [1,N], there are x-1 nums < x and N-x-1 nums > x, so # of combinations that m is the kth smallest num is C(x-1,k-1)*C(N-x-1,L-k):  choose k-1 nums from x-1 candidates + choose L-k nums from N-x-1 candidates. 

sum that x is the kth smallest = x*C(x-1,k-1)*C(N-x-1,L-k)

loop through all numbers in [1,N] to calculate all the sums

Q2:
number of routes without coins = no.of routes from (0,0) to (n,m) assuming there are no coins in grid - no.of routes from (0,0) to (n,m) that has coins

no.of routes from (0,0) to (n,m) that has coins:
for each coin in coins:
      count of routes with coin(x,y) = (routes from (0,0) to coin(x,y))*(routes from coin(x,y) to destination(n,m))

We can use combinatrics to find total routes from point (x,y) to (x1, y1) (Check this comment for derivation https://leetcode.com/discuss/interview-question/1648973/Sprinklr-or-on-campus-or-Intern/1194009)

There is a similar problem https://leetcode.com/discuss/interview-question/1648973/Sprinklr-or-on-campus-or-Intern, but the difference here is the constraint mentioned in this question, i. e one coin cannot reach another coin (read the constraint mentioned in the question) which means we dont need to worry about duplicate paths

The point in question 2 is that whatever path you consider from ```(1, 1)``` to ```(N, M)``` there would be **at most** one coin in the path because of the assumption of the problem in how coins are in the grid(```a<p, b>q or a>p,b<q```) and that allowable moves are to the right and bottom. So with this in mind, we can compute all paths that goes to ```(N, M)``` and have ```coin[i]``` in its path without any intersection with other coins much easier and after all we subtract these values from total paths.(As you may know the number of paths from ```(1, 1)``` to ```(N, M)``` without any restriction would be ```C(M-1, M+N-2)```)

Q2 combinations: https://leetcode.com/discuss/interview-question/1648973/Sprinklr-or-on-campus-or-Intern

we compute the routes with coins (5000 * 5000) rather than the standard element-wise dp (10 ** 5 * 10 ** 5)

total routes from 0,0 to (n-1,m-1)- total routes with coins is the answer for this question

from (x0,y0) to (x1,y1),there are C(x1-x0+y1-y0, x1-x0) ways. 

sort coins, so leftmost and topmost coins are calculated first

dp[i]=number of paths starting from (0,0) to obstacle[i] without passing through any obstacle.

dp[i]=(total path from (0,0) to obstacle[i])-sigma((total paths from obstacle[j] to obstacle[i) * dp[j]) 
 j = obstacle which is located just upper left of obstacle[i] which can be obtained by sorting obstacle array
